coredata.dc:title: Mesoporous silica-based molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for the ultrafast and sensitive recognition of oxytetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yeqing
Huang, Ting
Wang, Suao
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: Molecularly imprinted sensor has been a common chemical sensor for the selective detection of target. Yet, the rapid detection of target was one of the remaining problems, which needs to be diligently improved. Herein, a molecularly imprinted mesoporous silicon material (N-CDs@mMIPs) was successfully prepared for the rapid, sensitive and selective detection of oxytetracycline (OTC). Non-toxic nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) with high fluorescence performance was chosen as a detectable fluorescence signal, OTC was the target analysis, 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as the functional monomer, tetraethyl silicate (TEOS) as the crossing link agent and cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) as the pore-foaming agent. N-CDs@mMIPs was synthesized by the removal of CTAB and OTC. Under the optimal test conditions, the resultant N-CDs@mMIPs showed a faster response time within 0.5 min (30 s) and a lower detection limit of 0.035 μmol·L−1 compared with molecularly imprinted sensor without mesoporous structure (N-CDs@MIPs) and other molecularly imprinted sensors. Consequently, N-CDs@mMIPs was applied for the detection of OTC in milk and honey, satisfied recovery rates of 99.80 %–100.60 % and 99.75 %–102.10 % were obtained respectively. In general, the pore structure in mesoporous silicon accelerated the mass transfer rate between target and fluorescent substance, which greatly shortened the detection time of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC. Furthermore, the pore structure in mesoporous silicon could effectively avoid the self-aggregation of N-CDs, thus improved the sensitivity of N-CDs@mMIPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silicon
Rapid response
Oxytetracycline
Carbon quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Sensitive detection
Fluorescence analysis
Milk and honey

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation process and characterization of N-CDs@mMIPs
Optimization of conditions for OTC detection
Fluorescent detection of OTC with N-CDs@mMIPs
Comparisons of LOD and response time
Practical application in sample detection and recovery test

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (a, b)and TEM(c, d)images of N-CDs@mMIPs.

(a) Fluorescence intensity changes of N-CDs@mMIPs in 200 min, (b) The effect of the pH of the solution on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mMIPs, (c) The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mMIPs with 40 μmol L−1 OTC in 20 min (inset was the fluorescence intensity changes in 140 min).
Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs@mMIPs (a) and N-CDs@mNIPs (c) under different concentrations of OTC, the corresponding linear relationship diagram of N-CDs@mMIPs (b) and N-CDs@mNIPs (d).
The interference of common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl−, I−) in the detection environment on N-CDs@mMIPs.

Comparison of the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mMIPs (left) and N-CDs@mNIPs (right) to OTC and its similar drugs under the same concentration conditions (amoxicillin (A), azithromycin (B), erythromycin (C), streptomycin (D) and oxytetracycline (E)).

(a) The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@MIPs with 40 μmol L−1 OTC in 120 min; (b) fluorescence spectra of N-CDs@MIPs under different concentrations of OTC; (c) the corresponding linear relationship diagram of N-CDs@MIPs.

(a) The absorption spectra of N-CDs (green line), OTC (purple line) and the mixture of N-CDs and OTC (pink line), the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs (blue line); the transient fluorescence lifetime diagrams of N-CDs (b) and the mixture of N-CDs and OTC (c).

(a) and (b) The paramaters of HPLC-UV results and corresponding calibration curve for OTC; (c) The HPLC-UV profiles of milk and milk spiked with 20 μM TC; (d) The HPLC-UV profiles of honey and honey spiked with 20 μM OTC.
Flow chart of preparation of N-CDs@mMIPs.
Comparison of LOD and response time between N-CDs@mMIPs and other sensors.
Analysis comparison of the detection results of OTC in milk and honey samples with N-CDs@mMIPs and N-CDs@mNIPs (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Mesoporous silica-based molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor for the ultrafast and sensitive recognition of oxytetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: † Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, Advanced Chemical Engineering Laboratory of Green Materials and Energy of Jiangsu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly imprinted sensor has been a common chemical sensor for the selective detection of target. Yet, the rapid detection of target was one of the remaining problems, which needs to be diligently improved. Herein, a molecularly imprinted mesoporous silicon material (N-CDs@mMIPs) was successfully prepared for the rapid, sensitive and selective detection of oxytetracycline (OTC). Non-toxic nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) with high fluorescence performance was chosen as a detectable fluorescence signal, OTC was the target analysis, 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as the functional monomer, tetraethyl silicate (TEOS) as the crossing link agent and cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) as the pore-foaming agent. N-CDs@mMIPs was synthesized by the removal of CTAB and OTC. Under the optimal test conditions, the resultant N-CDs@mMIPs showed a faster response time within 0.5 min (30 s) and a lower detection limit of 0.035 μmol·L−1 compared with molecularly imprinted sensor without mesoporous structure (N-CDs@MIPs) and other molecularly imprinted sensors. Consequently, N-CDs@mMIPs was applied for the detection of OTC in milk and honey, satisfied recovery rates of 99.80 %–100.60 % and 99.75 %–102.10 % were obtained respectively. In general, the pore structure in mesoporous silicon accelerated the mass transfer rate between target and fluorescent substance, which greatly shortened the detection time of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC. Furthermore, the pore structure in mesoporous silicon could effectively avoid the self-aggregation of N-CDs, thus improved the sensitivity of N-CDs@mMIPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a fast-bacteriostatic agent with broad spectrum of anti-pathogenic microorganisms (Bai et al., 2020). It can not only promote the growth and reproduction of farmed animals, but also be used to treat and prevent infectious diseases between animals (Li et al., 2021). Because of its low cost and beneficial effect, it is widely added to animal feed, finally enters in human body through food chain and does harm to human health under the over-high concentration of OTC (Liu et al., 2017; Naik et al., 2017). Nowadays, the concentration of OTC has been strictly limited by World Health Organization and EU countries. Meanwhile, in China, the national standard (GB/T 22990−2008) stipulates that the OTC residue in milk shall not exceed 5.0 μg/kg; the national standard (GB/T 5009.116-2003) stipulates that the OTC residue in livestock and poultry shall not exceed 0.15 mg/kg; The national standard (GB/T 18932.23-2003) stipulates that the OTC residue in honey shall not exceed 0.001 mg/kg. At present, plenty of methods have been reported for the OTC detection, such as electrochemical analysis (Magesa et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021), aptamer analysis (Hou et al., 2013; Yuan et al., 2016), immunosorbent analysis (Hijaz and Killiny, 2020; Moumita et al., 2019), surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (González Fá et al., 2019; Meng et al., 2017)and high-performance liquid chromatography (Gajda et al., 2017), etc. Although the concentration of OTC can be detected by the analysis methods above, these methods still have some unavoidable defects, such as complex operating procedures and sample pre-processing procedures, expensive instruments, etc. Therefore, it is of quite necessary to find a convenient and effective analysis method for the OTC detection.

Molecularly imprinted fluorescence analysis is a method that specializes in selectively and sensitively detecting targets, which combines the selectivity of molecularly imprinted technology (MIT) and sensitivity of fluorescence analysis (Gui et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018) expertly. In recent years, many researchers have devoted themselves to the synthesis and optimization of molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors and have achieved satisfactory results. Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2018a) prepared a molecularly imprinted sensor based on allyl fluorescein for the sensitive and selective detection of tetracycline within 10 min. Luo et al. (Luo et al., 2019) designed a molecularly imprinted ratio fluorescence sensor based on quantum dots (QDs) for the simultaneous detection of hepatitis A and B viruses within 20 min. The research about molecularly imprinted sensors for the detection of OTC has also been reported recently. Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2019) synthesized a molecularly imprinted sensor which used CDs as fluorescent signal, then achieved selective and sensitive detection of OTC with a response time of 5.0 min. It can be found that the molecularly imprinted sensors generally exhibit a long response time to the target. Therefore, a molecularly imprinted sensor with rapid response time and high sensitivity must be the top priority for the researchers.

According to previous studies, doping metal ions or using surface imprinting technology can effectively accelerate the response time of target analysis (Gao et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2018a; Wei et al., 2014). However, the proposal of mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) provides another way to effectively accelerate the response time of target analysis. It is well known that MSNs possess larger specific surface area (Harraz et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020), which is beneficial for the target to enter the imprinting sites faster, thereby reducing the response time of the molecularly imprinted sensor to the target (Pan et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016). In another hand, embedding the fluorescent material in the MSNs can effectively overcome the fluorescence self-quenching phenomenon caused by the aggregation of the fluorescent materials, maximize the light response efficiency, and improve the detection limit of the sensor (Jiang et al., 2019). Fluorescence materials can be divided into three categories of dyes, rare earths and quantum dots (QDs), which provide detectable fluorescence signal for molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor. Among the three kinds of fluorescence materials, QDs have been widely used due to its high performance and easy modification (Hao et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2019). As one of the focal points of many quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) not only have the advantages of general quantum dots, but also are non-toxic (Chan et al., 2018; Jaleel and Pramod, 2018), so they have gradually become the favor object of researchers. However, CDs remains a problem of self-quenching by aggregation (Chen et al., 2016), which can be perfectly solved by the strategy of mesoporous silicon-coated.

In this work, the sol-gel method was applied to prepare the mesoporous molecularly imprinted polymer (N-CDs@mMIPs), in which the eco-friendly sol-gel method exhibited the advantages of easy reaction, simple and convenient operation among other common molecular imprinting polymerization methods, such as precipitation polymerization, in-situ polymerization, suspension polymerization, etc (Arabi et al., 2020a, b; Arabi et al., 2021a, b). The N-CDs@mMIPs based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) was successfully designed for the rapid and sensitive detection of OTC. It is conjectured that the pore structure of N-CDs@mMIPs would be helpful for the removal and rebinding of OTC, the dispersion of N-CDs. Moreover, N-CDs@mMIPs would be benefit to improve the light response efficiency of N-CDs to OTC (Jiang et al., 2019). To verify this conjecture, numerous characterizations were carried out to analyze the performance of N-CDs@mMIPs, the OTC in milk and honey samples was conducted to be detected by N-CDs@mMIPs, furthermore, the high-performance liquid chromatography was also carried out to verify the accuracy of fluorescence analysis method. The comparison of detection time and detection limit were implemented with other analysis methods. Thus, the application of MSNs by N-CDs@mMIPs greatly shorten the response time to targets and effectively improve the sensitivity of N-CDs@mMIPs. The proposal in this work must be a promising strategy for the rapid and sensitive detection of targets in the future or in many other research fields.

All reagents in the experiment were of analytical grade. Anhydrous citric acid, ammonia, tetraethyl silicate (TEOS), 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and trichloroacetic acid were obtained by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), sodium hydroxide, urea, hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Azithromycin, erythromycin, amoxicillin and streptomycin were purchased from the US Food and Drug Administration (Beijing, China). The water used in the whole experiment was double distilled water (DDW).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a quartz cell (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm). The size and structure of N-CDs@mMIPs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JSM-7001 F). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurement was carried out with a Themo Niclet NEXUS470 FT-IR spectrometer.

The preparation of N-CDs was based on some modifications of the reported literature (Wang et al., 2018b). The preparation process was as follows: 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL DDW. After stirring to form a uniform transparent solution, the mixture was transferred to a stainless-steel polytetrafluoroethylene lined reactor. The reactor was then placed in an oven at 160 ℃ for 4.0 h. After the reaction kettle was naturally cooled to room temperature, the dark green solution was obtained and centrifuged under 9000 rpm. Finally, non-toxic, non-polluting and high-fluorescence N-CDs were obtained and stored at 4.0 °C for further use.

N-CDs@mMIPs were prepared by a method previously reported with some modifications (Climent et al., 2010). In short, 1.0 mL N-CDs solution and 48 mL DDW were added to a 100 mL single neck round bottom flask. After uniformly stirring, 40 μL APTES and 46 mg OTC were added, the reaction was stirred for 2.0 h at room temperature. Then 100 mg CTAB, 200 μL NaOH (1.0 mol·L−1) was added and the solution was transferred to a water bath and reacted under 80 ℃ with magnetic stirring. Finally, the reaction was kept for 12 h after the addition of 400 μL TEOS and 400 μL ammonia dropwise. Then, the template molecule OTC and the porogen CTAB were washed alternately with water and ethanol for five times under ultrasonic conditions (Amjadi and Jalili, 2017; Climent et al., 2010). Finally, the products were dried in a vacuum at 60 ℃ for 24 h to obtain N-CDs@mMIPs with uniform pore structure and special recognize sites of OTC. For comparison, a mesoporous non-imprinted fluorescent polymer (N-CDs@mNIPs) was prepared by the same method and the same washing method without the addition of template molecule OTC. The OTC imprinted fluorescent polymer (N-CDs@MIPs) was prepared by the same method and the same washing method without the addition of CTAB.

During the experiment, all the fluorescent detections were performed under the same conditions, and all the fluorescence spectra were obtained by molecular fluorescence spectrophotometer (F98). The fluorescence spectra were obtained at a wavelength range of 350−650 nm, and the excitation wavelength was set at 345 nm. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube was 750 V, the scanning speed was 1000 nm min−1, the scanning interval was 1.0 nm, and the slit width of excitation and emission were all of 10 nm. N-CDs@mMIPs, N-CDs@mNIPs and N-CDs@MIPs were dissolved in DDW to obtain fresh stock solutions (200 mg·L−1). The OTC stock solution (1.0 mmol·L−1 in DDW) was kept at 4.0 ℃ for further use. At room temperature, an equal amount of N-CDs@mMIPs, N-CDs@mNIPs and N-CDs@MIPs solutions were added to a 10 mL colorimetric tube, and different concentrations of OTC standard solutions were added in sequence. Then the solutions were placed in colorimetric tubes to wait for a while. Finally, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs, N-CDs@mNIPs and N-CDs@MIPs were measured and recorded.

The milk and honey samples were purchased from a local supermarket. In order to remove a large amount of protein and fat in milk, 2.0 mL milk, 2.0 mL DDW and 1.0 mL 10 % trichloroacetic acid were added to a 10 mL centrifuge tube. Then the resulting mixture was sonicated for 30 min, and finally centrifuged at a centrifugal speed of 10000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred to another centrifuge tube, centrifuged again under the same conditions, and then filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The honey was diluted with phosphate buffer (200 g·L−1) and sonicated for 30 min. Then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Finally, the obtained milk and honey samples were stored at 4.0 ℃. Before detection, the pH of the actual milk and honey sample solutions were adjusted to optimal detection pH (optimal pH = 6.0) with 1.0 M HCl in advance.

The HPLC analysis method referred to the master's thesis of Tan Wang of Beijing University of Chemical Technology in 2006 with some modifications. Specially, a HPLC (Agilent 1260 Infinity II) with Shim-pack GIST C18 reversed-phase column (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm) were used for chromatographic separation at the room temperature. The mobile phase A was an acetic acid solution with pH = 4.0, and the mobile phase B was an acetonitrile solution. Under the condition of a UV detector of 275 nm, the gradient elution method shown in the following table was used. The flow rate was set at 0.7 mL min−1 and the injected volume of samples was 20 μL with a detection time of 16 min. The sample preparation method of milk and honey were similar to that of fluorescence analysis. Finally, the actual samples were sampling and testing by HPLC analysis.
Time
Ratio 0 9.0 13 16 16.01
A 85 % 70 % 15 % 85 % Stop
B 15 % 30 % 85 % 15 % Stop

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process and characterization of N-CDs@mMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs@mMIPs were synthesized by sol-gel method. The synthesis process was shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the N-CDs were fully dispersed in the solution, the monomer APTES and template molecule OTC were added to start the pre-reaction, then the hole generating template CTAB was add for blending. Next, TEOS and ammonia were added when the temperature reached to 80 ℃. Finally, CTAB and OTC were eluted to obtain N-CDs@mMIPs with porous structure.

The morphological structure of N-CDs@mMIPs was characterized by SEM and TEM techniques. It could be seen from the SEM images (Fig. 1 a and b) that N-CDs@mMIPs presented a good spherical shape. The TEM images (Fig. 1c and d) showed that N-CDs@mMIPs were mesoporous spheres with a particle size of about 230 nm, the ordered pores could be clearly observed from the TEM images. The TEM images were a strong evidence for the successful preparation of N-CDs@mMIPs.

The FI-IR spectrum of N-CDs@mMIPs was showed in Fig. 2 , the peaks at 463 cm−1 and 797 cm−1 were the characteristic signals of SiO symmetric stretching vibration. The peak at 1080 cm−1 was asymmetric stretching of SiOSi. The characteristic peak at 1490 cm−1 was the flexural vibration peak of NH, which indicated the presence of the functional monomer APTES in N-CDs@mMIPs. The peak at 1650 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN bond. The characteristic peak at 2920 cm−1 was stretching vibration of CH. The FIIR results also reflected the successful synthesis of N-CDs@mMIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for OTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Before using N-CDs@mMIPs to detect OTC, the stability, reaction time and the best pH testing value of N-CDs@mMIPs must be studied and optimized.

Firstly, the stability of N-CDs@mMIPs was studied in a certain period. The fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs (200 mg L−1 stock solution) in different time were determined and recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 a, 30 measurements were taken within 200 min. It could be seen from the figure that the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs had almost remained relatively stable. The experimental results showed that the prepared N-CDs@mMIPs possessed good stability and could be well applied to actual detection.

Secondly, the influence of the pH ranged from 1.0–14 on the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs and the detection system was studied. It could be found in Fig. 3b that when the pH of the solution at 6.0, the fluorescence intensity reached to the maximum. When the solution was over acidic, it had a great impact on the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs; while the solution was over alkaline, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs reduced a bit, not much compared to the over-acid environment. Therefore, the experimental results indicated that pH = 6.0 was the best solution pH value in this experiment.

Finally, the best response time of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC was studied and showed in Fig. 3c, 40 μmol L−1 OTC was added to the N-CDs@mMIPs solution, the fluorescence spectra were measured and recorded at different time intervals. Fig. 3c showed the fluorescence intensity changes in the first 20 min, the inset of Fig. 3c was the fluorescence intensity changes in 140 min. It could be clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mMIPs decreased rapidly within the first 0.5 min. After 0.5 min, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mMIPs remained almost unchanged. Therefore, 0.5 min was chosen as the best response time for the next fluorescent detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of OTC with N-CDs@mMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent detection ability of N-CDs@mMIPs was studied. At room temperature, different concentrations of OTC solutions and N-CDs@mMIPs were mixed to a certain volume to form a test solution, and then transferred to a quartz cuvette for the following fluorescent detection.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the increasing concentrations of OTC from 0−100 μmol L−1, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs at 447 nm gradually decreased. By calculating the changes of fluorescence intensities and OTC concentrations, the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity changes of N-CDs@mMIPs and OTC concentrations were obtained. As shown in Fig. 4b, the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity changes of N-CDs@mMIPs and OTC concentrations was y = 0.00884x-0.02867, where "y" represented the ordinate (F0-F)/F0, and "x" was the OTC concentrations on the abscissa C, the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.99672, the detection limit (3σ/k) was calculated to 0.035 μmol·L−1, where k and σ represented to the slope of the calibration line and the standard deviation of the blank measurement (n = 11) respectively. For a comparison, Fig. 4c showed the fluorescence intensity spectra of N-CDs@mNIPs with different OTC concentrations. It could be found in Fig. 4c that as the OTC concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mNIPs decreased slowly and had only a tiny degree of quenching. Fig. 4d was the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity changes of N-CDs@mNIPs and OTC concentrations. The linear correlation equation of N-CDs@mNIPs was y = 0.00265x-0.0002 (R2 = 0.9973), in which the slope obviously decreased compared with N-CDs@mMIPs. The reason for this result was mainly attributed to the abundant binding sites matched with OTC in N-CDs@mMIPs, while there were no special recognition sites of OTC in N-CDs@mNIPs, so only a little fluorescence quenching was caused on N-CDs@mNIPs. The experimental results forcefully proved the specific recognition ability of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC.

In order to further explore the performance of N-CDs@mMIPs when applied to the actual environmental detection, the influence of possible common ions on N-CDs@mMIPs was analyzed. Several potential common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl−, I−) in milk and honey were selected for research. The fluorescence intensities of N-CDs@mMIPs solutions containing different ions were measured and listed in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5, the result clearly stated that the presence of the possible common ions in the detection environment had almost no effect on the detection of OTC by N-CDs@mMIPs. Thus, the N-CDs@mMIPs prepared in the experiment had essential anti-interference ability and was suitable for the application of actual samples detection.

In order to study the selectivity of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC, some similar drugs (amoxicillin (A), azithromycin (B), erythromycin (C), streptomycin (D)) were selected to explore the effect on N-CDs@mMIPs together with oxytetracycline (E). At the same time, the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs@mMIPs and N-CDs@mNIPs were recorded when various drugs were added. As shown in Fig. 6 , compared with other similar drugs under the same concentrations, OTC had the most obvious quenching effect on N-CDs@mMIPs, the F0/F value of N-CDs@mMIPs reached to 2.94, which is nearly three times that of other analogs and N-CDs@mNIPs. This was mainly due to the large number of imprinting sites that could specifically recognize OTC in N-CDs@mMIPs. When the OTC was added, these specific recognition sites could quickly recombine with OTC and caused rapid fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. While the OTC similar drugs could not form a perfect complementary relationship with the imprinting sites in N-CDs@mMIPs. Therefore, it would not cause obvious fluorescence quenching of N-CDs@mMIPs. At the same time, the selectivity of N-CDs@mNIPs was studied under the same conditions. OTC and other similar drugs had almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mNIPs. The experimental results further showed that after the imprinting process, N-CDs@mMIPs had specific recognition ability for OTC, while had no specific recognition ability for other similar drugs. It also demonstrated that N-CDs@mNIPs without any imprinting process had no specific recognition ability among any targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparisons of LOD and response time
ce:section.ce:para: In order to compare the effect of CTAB on the response time and detection limit of the target more intuitively, N-CDs@MIPs was introduced to detect the response time and detection limit for comparison. As displayed in Fig. 7 , the response time of N-CDs@MIPs was 5.0 min, which was much slower than that of N-CDs@mMIPs. The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs@MIPs exposed to the same range of OTC were showed in Fig. 7b, the corresponding linear fitting equation was displayed in Fig. 7c, the equation was y = 0.00839x+0.03004 (R2 = 0.9978), the LOD of N-CDs@MIPs was calculated to 0.049 μmol L−1. Then compared the LOD and response time of N-CDs@mMIPs with N-CDs@MIPs, the LOD of N-CDs@mMIPs was increased by 1.4 times, and the response speed to the target was increased by 9.0 time. This indicated that the mesoporous structure could greatly improve the light response efficiency, thereby improving the sensitivity of target detection, and the polymers with mesoporous structure could shorten the detection time of the targets to a certain extent. In another hand, the comparison of LOD and response time between N-CDs@mMIPs and other sensors were conducted and displayed in Table 1 , compared with other sensors, N-CDs@mMIPs also owned the lower LOD and faster detection time. Therefore, the synthesis of MIPs with mesoporous structure could effectively solve the problems of low sensitivity and slow response time in traditional MIPs.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs to OTC was finally explored to further elaborate the interaction between N-CDs and OTC. As displayed in Fig. 8 a, the absorption of N-CDs (green line), OTC (purple line) and the mixture of N-CDs with OTC (pink line) were showed that there was no obvious shift in the position of the absorption peaks of N-CDs and OTC, which might not be the internal filtration effect (Xu et al., 2022). The emission of N-CDs was also showed as the blue line in Fig. 8a, the emission of N-CDs did not overlap with the absorption peak of OTC, so its fluorescence quenching mechanism could not be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Ma et al., 2017). Then the transient fluorescence lifetime diagrams of N-CDs and the mixture of N-CDs and OTC were exhibited in Fig. 8b and c, the second-order fit was selected to be the best fit formula (Liu et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2018). Finally, the τ0 and τ1 were calculated to 7.53 ns and 6.21 ns respectively, τ0/τ1 ≠ 1, this result indicated that the quenching mechanism couldn’t be static quenching. Thus, the most possible quenching mechanism must be dynamic quenching, in which the measured linear equation was consistent with a dynamic Stern-Volmer quenching mechanism (Xu et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in sample detection and recovery test
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, the prepared N-CDs@mMIPs and N-CDs@mNIPs were used to detect OTC in the actual samples of milk and honey samples by using the spike recovery method. The experimental results obtained were shown in Table 2 . The recovery rates of OTC in milk and honey samples, which obtained by N-CDs@mMIPs were 99.80 %∼100.60 % and 99.75 %∼102.10 %, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of milk and honey detection were 1.13 %∼1.25 % and 1.12 %∼1.47 %, respectively. The corresponding recovery rates of OTC in milk and honey samples, which obtained by N-CDs@mNIPs were 82.10 %∼100.60 % and 88.40 %∼95.25 %, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of milk and honey detection were 2.13 %∼2.87 % and 2.22 %∼2.94 %, respectively. Furthermore, the milk and honey samples were detected by HPLC analysis method, the detection results were showed in Fig. 9 and the specific results comparisons were displayed in Table S1. Comparing the detection results of N-CDs@mMIPs and N-CDs@mNIPs with HPLC analysis, it could be seen that the detection results of N-CDs@mMIPs were more accurate and reliable. This might because of the more obvious response of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@mMIPs decreased more than that of N-CDs@mNIPs at the same concentration. So, the inevitable error and accidental error generated by N-CDs@mMIPs were smaller. The test detection experiments strongly showed that N-CDs@mMIPs could be successfully applied for the detection and analysis of milk and honey samples.

In summary, N-CDs@mMIPs were successfully synthesized and used to rapidly and selectively detect OTC in food samples. Compared with common silicon-based molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor, N-CDs@mMIPs with mesoporous structure had a larger specific surface area, which effectively enhanced the light response efficiency of N-CDs and greatly improved the response rate of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC. The fluorescence stability, pH effect, response time, ions interference, selective experiments and actual sample detection experiments were conducted to measure the detection performance and analysis feasibility of N-CDs@mMIPs to OTC. The results showed that N-CDs@mMIPs owned the excellent capability for the detection of OTC and a promising potential for the OTC detection in actual samples. Furthermore, the HPLC analysis was carried out to verify the accuracy of fluorescence analysis for OTC by N-CDs@mMIPs and satisfied results were obtained. Thus, the strategy of synthesizing MSNs can not only effectively solve the problem of too long response time of imprinted polymers to the targets, but also improve the light response efficiency of N-CDs, thereby increasing the detection limit of the imprinted polymers. This provides a good idea for rapid and highly sensitive detection of targets in the field of analysis and detection.

Yeqing Xu: Data curation, Writing-Original draft preparation, Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Ting Huang: Analysis and testing, supplementary data. Suao Wang: Chart optimization, experiment optimization. Yongsheng Yan: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Reviewing and Editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21808089), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019M661750, 2019T120400), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20190245), Major projects of education department of Jiangsu province (No. 17KJA530003), Programs of Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 15JDG024).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2022.104427.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article: The fluorescence analysis and HPLC analysis and test results comparison of OTC in milk and honey samples.
